That underline exercise sounds useful. But I worry that if I remove too many “academic” signals, it will sound unsophisticated. How do you keep it sounding scholarly?

Scholarly tone comes less from Latinate vocabulary and more from (a) precision, (b) disciplined qualification, and (c) clear relationships between claims and evidence. You can sound highly academic while using mostly plain words. Ways to keep a scholarly register without jargon: 1) Use technical terms only where they do real work - “Binary” might be a real concept; “reify” might be necessary in some theories; “problematize” often isn’t. - If a term is theory-loaded, keep it—but define it once and then use it consistently. 2) Replace “inflation” with “specificity” - Instead of “interrogate,” say what kind of analysis: “analyze,” “trace,” “compare,” “model,” “test,” “evaluate,” “interpret.” These are scholarly because they name methods of thinking. 3) Qualify with scope, not fog - Foggy hedge: “It is arguably somewhat possible that…” - Precise hedge: “In contexts where X holds, Y tends to occur.” That’s more academic because it states conditions. 4) Use explicit causal and contrastive logic - “Because,” “therefore,” “however,” “by contrast,” “consequently.” Readers experience this as rigorous because it shows inference. 5) Use noun phrases strategically, not habitually - Nominalizations can be useful for abstraction (“legitimation,” “measurement”), but don’t chain them. 6) Add intellectual scaffolding through structure - A clear roadmap sentence at the end of an introduction is “academic” because it signals argument architecture. If you share what kind of writing you do (e.g., theoretical argument, empirical report, literature review), I can tailor the advice. Different genres tolerate different densities.
Let’s say it’s mostly literature review + argument, not a methods-heavy report. I synthesize positions and then argue for a framing.

For literature review + argument, clarity comes from making relationships between sources explicit. Dense reviews often summarize source A, then source B, but leave the reader to infer the comparison. You can stay scholarly by writing the “relational” sentences more explicitly. A high-clarity literature review typically does: 1) Establishes the question or tension driving the review. 2) Groups sources by position (not by chronology or author-by-author). 3) Uses “moves” verbs: “argues,” “assumes,” “defines,” “extends,” “complicates,” “contrasts,” “critiques.” 4) Ends sections with synthesis: what the cluster implies for your claim. Concrete templates (adapt as needed): - Positioning: “Scholars disagree about X. One line of work treats X as ____, while another treats it as ____.” - Synthesis: “Taken together, these accounts suggest ____. However, they leave unresolved ____.” - Gap/need: “Existing work explains ____, but it has not fully addressed ____.” - Your move: “I build on ____, but I depart from ____ by ____.” Micro-level clarity moves in lit review: - Start paragraphs with the synthesis claim, then cite multiple sources as support (rather than starting with citations). - Use “This view” / “This approach” to refer back, but ensure the referent is unambiguous. - Limit each paragraph to one comparative axis (e.g., definitions, mechanisms, implications). If you want, we can take a hypothetical set of 3–4 “source claims” and practice turning them into a synthesized paragraph that’s formal but readable.

Okay. Suppose Source 1 says clarity is about reader accessibility, Source 2 says clarity is ideological and hides power, Source 3 says clarity is a skill that can be taught without sacrificing complexity. How do I synthesize?

Here’s a synthesized paragraph that makes the relationships explicit and ends with a tension you can use for your argument: “Accounts of clarity in academic writing diverge in how they define its purpose and effects. Some treat clarity primarily as reader accessibility: writing is clear when it enables readers to follow an argument with minimal unnecessary effort. Others argue that appeals to clarity are ideological, functioning as a normative demand that can discipline style and marginalize forms of expression associated with particular communities or traditions. A third line of work reframes clarity as a teachable rhetorical skill, emphasizing that writers can increase comprehensibility while still presenting complex claims and acknowledging uncertainty. Read together, these approaches suggest that clarity is not a single standard but a contested practice—one that can support understanding while also serving as a gatekeeping criterion. This tension motivates a more precise question: what kinds of clarity are being promoted, by whom, and with what evaluative consequences?” Why this works: - Opens with a “diverge” sentence that sets the comparative frame. - Uses “Some… Others… A third…” to group positions. - Gives each position one sentence of fair summary. - Ends with synthesis + a question that sets up your next section. To keep it scholarly, notice the verbs: “define,” “treat,” “argue,” “function,” “reframe,” “emphasize.” Those are analytic actions, not decorative words.

That helps. My problem is that when I try to write those “read together” sentences, I end up with vague phrases like “This indicates the complexity of the issue.” How do I make synthesis sentences specific?

A synthesis sentence becomes specific when it does at least one of these things: - names the exact point of agreement/disagreement, - identifies an underlying assumption that differs, - specifies what each view predicts or implies, - states what remains unresolved. Instead of “This indicates complexity,” use one of these targeted synthesis patterns: 1) Agreement + divergence: “While all three accounts treat clarity as consequential, they disagree about whether its primary function is communicative (accessibility) or regulatory (norm enforcement).” 2) Assumption-level synthesis: “These positions hinge on different assumptions about audiences: one imagines a general reader, another focuses on institutional gatekeepers, and the third centers the writer’s trainable choices.” 3) Implication-level synthesis: “If clarity is framed as accessibility, then pedagogy should emphasize reader cues and organization; if it is framed as ideology, then pedagogy must also address whose norms are being taught.” 4) Unresolved question: “However, existing work rarely specifies how to distinguish ‘clarity as comprehension’ from ‘clarity as conformity’ in actual evaluation practices.” 5) Conditional synthesis (good for nuance): “Clarity may enhance understanding in contexts where readers share background knowledge, yet it may also enforce conformity when evaluators treat one style as universally neutral.” Exercise: after summarizing positions, ask: - “What do these views disagree about?” (definition, mechanism, ethics, practice) - “What would each view recommend we do?” Then write the synthesis sentence as an answer, not as a placeholder.
I also get feedback about “too many prepositional phrases” and “noun strings.” Can you explain what to look for and how to fix it?

Prepositional phrases (“of,” “in,” “for,” “with,” “by,” “to”) and noun strings (“evaluation regime incentive structure”) often signal that you’re compressing relationships that should be expressed with verbs. What to look for: 1) Chains of “of” - Dense: “the evaluation of the effectiveness of the implementation of the policy” - Fix by adding verbs: “We evaluated how effective the policy was after it was implemented.” 2) “in relation to / with respect to / in terms of” These often mean “about,” “for,” “when,” or “because.” - Dense: “in relation to evaluative regimes” - Clear: “under evaluation systems” or “when evaluators…” 3) Noun strings (multiple nouns in a row) - Dense: “graduate student writing clarity improvement strategies” - Clear: “strategies for improving the clarity of graduate students’ writing” Or better: “strategies that help graduate students write more clearly” How to fix systematically: Step A: Find the head noun In “evaluative regimes that incentivize the proliferation of specialized terminology,” the head noun is “regimes.” Ask: what do regimes do? Answer with a verb: “Evaluation systems encourage…” Step B: Convert one noun into a verb - “proliferation” → “spread” or “increase” - “incentivize” → “encourage” (often enough) Step C: Replace abstract containers with concrete agents when appropriate - “The literature suggests…” can be fine, but often you can specify: “Several studies show…” A quick diagnostic: if a sentence has 4+ prepositional phrases in a row, try rewriting it with at least one main verb that carries the action.
Sometimes I’m told my writing has “too much metadiscourse,” like “This paper will argue” and “It is important to note.” But I thought signposting was good?

Signposting is good when it provides navigation or clarifies stakes. It becomes clutter when it repeats obvious moves or delays the content. Two categories: 1) Useful metadiscourse (keep or refine) - Roadmaps that orient the reader: “I first outline X, then show Y, and finally argue Z.” - Scope and limits: “This analysis focuses on ____, not ____.” - Definitions: “By ‘clarity,’ I mean ____.” 2) Low-value metadiscourse (usually cut) - Throat-clearing: “It is important to note that…” “It should be mentioned that…” - Self-narration that replaces claims: “This paper will argue that clarity matters.” (Instead: “Clarity matters because…”) - Empty emphasis: “Clearly,” “obviously,” “it is evident that” (unless you’re deliberately signaling shared premises) How to revise: - Replace “This paper argues that X” with “X” + reason. - Replace “It is important to note that Y” with “Y” + consequence. Example: “It is important to note that evaluation shapes style.” → “Evaluation shapes style, because reviewers often treat jargon as expertise.” A good rule: if the sentence can be deleted without changing the argument’s content, it’s probably low-value metadiscourse.
I’m also unsure about hedging. I don’t want to sound overconfident, but too many “may,” “might,” and “arguably” makes it weak.

Hedging is a precision tool: it should match the strength of your evidence and the scope of your claim. Weakness happens when hedges accumulate or when they hedge the wrong thing (the claim rather than the conditions). Strategies: 1) Hedge once per claim, not three times - Over-hedged: “It might be argued that X may potentially suggest…” - Better: “These patterns suggest X.” (One hedge: “suggest”) Or: “In these cases, X tends to…” 2) Move hedges into scope conditions - Instead of: “X may be true.” - Write: “X is true when A and B hold.” This reads more confident and more rigorous. 3) Use stronger verbs with bounded scope - “indicates” (strong) vs “suggests” (moderate) vs “is consistent with” (cautious) Choose based on evidence. 4) Differentiate epistemic vs rhetorical hedges - Epistemic: uncertainty in the world/evidence (“likely,” “possibly”) - Rhetorical politeness: softening disagreement (“I propose,” “I suggest”) Use rhetorical softeners sparingly; let the evidence carry the caution. 5) Use citations as a hedge Sometimes you can reduce hedging by attributing: “Several scholars argue that…” Now you’re not claiming certainty; you’re reporting a position. If you give me two example sentences (invented is fine) where you tend to over-hedge, I can show how to calibrate them.

Invented examples: 1) “It could perhaps be argued that clarity might in some cases function as a mechanism of exclusion.” 2) “This study may potentially contribute to ongoing debates regarding the role of accessibility in scholarly communication.”

Revisions with calibrated hedging: 1) Original: “It could perhaps be argued that clarity might in some cases function as a mechanism of exclusion.” Better options: - Moderate: “In some contexts, appeals to clarity function as a mechanism of exclusion.” - More cautious: “In some contexts, appeals to clarity can function as a mechanism of exclusion.” - Attribution hedge: “Some scholars argue that appeals to clarity can function as a mechanism of exclusion.” What changed: removed stacked hedges (“could perhaps,” “might”) and kept one scope hedge (“in some contexts”) plus optionally “can.” 2) Original: “This study may potentially contribute to ongoing debates regarding the role of accessibility in scholarly communication.” Better options: - Direct: “This study contributes to debates about the role of accessibility in scholarly communication.” - Cautious but not mushy: “This study contributes modestly to debates about…” - Specific contribution: “This study clarifies how accessibility is operationalized in evaluations of scholarly communication.” If you worry about overclaiming, specificity is safer than hedging. Saying exactly what you contribute (“clarifies definitions,” “compares frameworks,” “identifies tensions”) is both modest and confident.
What about passive voice? I’ve heard conflicting advice. Some say avoid it, others say it’s fine.

Passive voice is fine when it serves a purpose. The problem is habitual passive that hides agents or makes sentences harder to parse. Use passive when: - the agent is unknown/irrelevant: “The term is used inconsistently.” - you want to foreground the object/topic: “Clarity is often treated as…” - you’re describing conventional procedures and the actor doesn’t matter (genre-dependent). Prefer active when: - agency matters for your argument: “Reviewers reward dense prose” (agent = reviewers) - you’re making causal claims about who does what: “Evaluation systems encourage jargon.” A practical test: If you can add “by zombies” to the end and it’s meaningful, it’s passive. Ask: do you need to name who did it? If yes, go active. Example: “Density becomes valorized.” → By whom? If it matters: “Reviewers and editors often valorize density.” So: don’t ban passive; use it intentionally. In argument-driven writing, active voice often improves clarity because it makes causal chains explicit.
I struggle with transitions. I know the ideas in my head, but on the page the reader gets lost. How do I write better transitions between paragraphs?

Paragraph transitions work best when they do two things at once: (1) point back to what you just established, and (2) point forward to the next step. Think “backward hook + forward hinge.” A reliable transition recipe: 1) First sentence of new paragraph: refer back in a specific way - “This view of clarity as accessibility…” - “However, this emphasis on accessibility…” Avoid vague “This” without a noun. 2) Same sentence or second sentence: state what changes now (contrast, extension, application) - Contrast: “However, it overlooks…” - Extension: “Building on this, I show…” - Application: “To see how this works in practice, consider…” Examples: - Contrast transition: “Framing clarity as accessibility highlights the reader’s experience. However, it does not explain why certain styles are treated as ‘clear’ in evaluation settings. The next section addresses clarity as a normative standard.” - Extension transition: “Accounts that treat clarity as ideological show how norms can discipline style. Building on this insight, I distinguish between clarity that improves comprehension and clarity that enforces conformity.” - Zoom-in transition: “Debates about clarity often remain abstract. To make the stakes concrete, I examine the language evaluators use when they praise or criticize ‘clarity.’” Also: end paragraphs with a “launcher” sentence that sets up the next paragraph. That way you don’t have to do all the work at the beginning of the next one.
Can you show how to end a paragraph with a launcher sentence without sounding like a cheesy cliffhanger?

A launcher doesn’t need drama; it just needs direction. The key is to name the next analytical task plainly. Examples of non-cheesy launchers: - Method/next step: “To evaluate this claim, the next section compares how each framework defines the reader.” - Gap: “Yet these accounts do not specify how clarity is measured in practice, a question I take up next.” - Narrowing: “This suggests that the debate turns less on clarity itself than on who sets the standard; the next section examines those standards.” - Application: “Seeing clarity as a contested practice raises a further issue: how do evaluators operationalize it when judging writing?” - Stakes: “If clarity operates as a gatekeeping norm, then teaching clarity is not just a stylistic matter but an ethical one. The next section explores that implication.” All of these are “academic launchers”: they’re explicit about the work to come, not performative suspense.
Another issue: I often define a term in a long sentence with lots of parentheses and caveats. Any tips for cleaner definitions?

Cleaner definitions come from separating (1) the core definition, (2) boundary cases, and (3) implications. A three-sentence definition pattern: 1) Core: “By X, I mean ____.” 2) Contrast: “This differs from Y, which ____.” 3) Scope: “In this discussion, X refers to ____; it does not include ____.” Example for “clarity”: “By ‘clarity,’ I mean writing that enables readers to identify the main claim, follow the inferential steps, and understand key terms with minimal unnecessary effort. This differs from simplification, which reduces complexity by omitting qualifications or alternative interpretations. In this discussion, clarity refers to rhetorical and structural choices rather than to a single standardized style.” If you need caveats, put them in the scope sentence, not inside the core definition. Parentheses are often a sign you’re trying to do all three jobs at once.
How do I know when a technical term is necessary versus just jargon?

A technical term is necessary when it increases precision more than it increases cognitive load. Three tests: 1) Irreplaceability test Can you replace it with a common word without losing an important distinction? - “Validity” vs “accuracy”: not identical; “validity” may be necessary. - “Problematize” vs “question”: often replaceable. 2) Reusability test Will you use the term repeatedly as a stable concept? If yes, define it once and keep it. If it appears once for flair, it’s likely jargon. 3) Audience test Will your intended readers already know it? If not, can you define it quickly without derailing? If it’s crucial but unfamiliar, define it succinctly and give an example. Also watch for “prestige synonyms”: words that sound technical but don’t sharpen meaning (e.g., “utilize” for “use,” “ameliorate” for “improve” unless you need the specific nuance).
I’m trying to develop a revision process. When I draft, it’s messy. When I revise, I get overwhelmed. What’s a practical workflow?

Use a staged workflow that separates idea-level revision from sentence-level editing. Overwhelm happens when you try to fix everything simultaneously. A practical 4-pass workflow: Pass 1: Argument map (global) - For each section: write one sentence: “This section does X.” - For each paragraph: write one sentence: “This paragraph claims Y.” If you can’t, restructure before line-editing. Pass 2: Paragraph architecture (meso) For each paragraph, check: - Topic sentence states the claim (not just the topic). - Evidence/examples/citations support that claim. - Last sentence links forward. Pass 3: Sentence clarity (local) Target common density drivers: - nominalizations - noun strings - long lead-ins - stacked hedges - ambiguous “this/it” Pass 4: Style polish (surface) - vary sentence length - check transitions - remove redundancy - tighten wordiness Time-saving rule: do not do Pass 3 or 4 until Pass 1 and 2 are stable. Otherwise you’ll polish sentences you later delete. If you want, I can give you a checklist you can print and run through quickly.
Yes, a checklist would be great. Also, how do I measure if a paragraph is “clear enough”?

Checklist: Global (section level) - [ ] Can I state my thesis in one sentence with a “because” clause? - [ ] Does each section have a distinct job that advances the thesis? - [ ] Are key terms defined before heavy use? Paragraph level - [ ] Does the first sentence make a claim (not just announce a topic)? - [ ] Can I summarize the paragraph’s point in 10–15 words? - [ ] Are citations grouped to support a synthesis claim rather than listed sequentially? - [ ] Does the paragraph end by stating what follows from it? Sentence level - [ ] Is the main verb close to the subject (no long detours)? - [ ] Did I avoid stacking more than one hedge per claim? - [ ] Did I convert at least one nominalization into a verb where possible? - [ ] Are pronouns (“this,” “it,” “they”) unambiguous? - [ ] Did I cut filler (“it is important to note,” “in terms of,” “with respect to”)? Word/phrase level - [ ] Are key terms consistent (no unnecessary synonyms)? - [ ] Are sentences free of unnecessary intensifiers (“very,” “extremely”)? - [ ] Did I eliminate prestige synonyms (“utilize”) unless needed? How to measure “clear enough”: 1) The “skeptical reader” test Imagine a reader asking after each paragraph: “So what?” “Compared to what?” “Why should I believe this?” If your paragraph pre-empts at least one of these, clarity is improving. 2) The “reverse outline” test After drafting, write the point of each paragraph in the margin. If two adjacent paragraphs have the same point, or if one paragraph has two points, clarity will suffer. 3) The “stress test” reading Read the paragraph aloud and mark where you run out of breath or lose track. Those spots usually need either a sentence break or a clearer subject-verb spine. 4) The “concept count” test If a sentence introduces more than ~2 new abstract concepts, consider splitting it. If you want a more quantitative proxy, you can check average sentence length and nominalization frequency, but the reverse outline is usually the best indicator for argument clarity.
You mentioned nominalization frequency. How do I spot nominalizations quickly, and are there cases where they’re actually better?

Quick spotting: Nominalizations often end in suffixes like: - -tion / -sion (implementation, evaluation) - -ment (development, management) - -ance / -ence (reliance, difference) - -ity (clarity, legitimacy) - -ism (formalism) - -ization (operationalization) They aren’t “bad” by default. They’re useful when: 1) You need to refer to a process as an object so you can analyze it: “The evaluation of clarity varies across contexts.” Here “evaluation” is the topic. 2) You need abstraction for conceptual work: “Legitimacy” can be a legitimate (no pun intended) analytic object. 3) You want cohesion: If the previous sentence introduced a process, nominalizing it can help you refer back: “Reviewers reward dense prose. This reward structure shapes…” The problem is when nominalizations pile up and hide action: “the facilitation of the development of…” Fix by restoring verbs and agents: “X helps develop Y.” A targeted approach: - Keep nominalizations when they are your topic. - Convert them when they are just carrying the sentence’s action.
I also tend to overuse “which” and “that” clauses. Any advice?

Overuse of relative clauses often means you’re trying to attach too many ideas to one noun. Two fixes: 1) Turn some clauses into separate sentences Dense: “Clarity, which is often framed as accessibility, which in turn is associated with…” Clear: “Clarity is often framed as accessibility. This framing is associated with…” 2) Use selective relatives: only attach what’s essential - “that” (restrictive) is usually essential to identify the noun: “The criteria that evaluators use…” - “which” (nonrestrictive) often adds extra info: “The criteria, which vary across contexts,…” If the “which” clause is crucial, it probably deserves its own sentence. Also watch for “which” referring to an entire previous clause—this can create ambiguity. Ambiguous: “Reviewers reward jargon, which reinforces inequity.” Does “which” refer to rewarding jargon or jargon itself? Clear: “This practice reinforces inequity.” A good practice: if you have more than one “which” in a sentence, rewrite.
I want to keep some complexity, though. I don’t want everything to sound like a textbook.

Complexity can come from the ideas and the structure of the argument, not from syntactic entanglement. You can write sentences that are clear yet layered. Tools for “clear complexity”: - Use one main clause + one well-placed subordinate clause (not three). Example: “Although clarity is often framed as accessibility, this framing does not account for how evaluators treat style as evidence of expertise.” That’s complex but trackable. - Use parallel structure to compare positions: “Where one approach treats clarity as comprehension, another treats it as conformity.” - Use controlled contrast markers: “However,” “yet,” “by contrast,” “nevertheless.” These let you articulate nuance without nested clauses. - Use examples as complexity carriers: Abstract nuance becomes clearer when anchored. You can also vary register: keep the core claims in simple syntax, and allow slightly more elaborate sentences for interpretation—so the reader always has a foothold.
Can we do an exercise? Give me a dense sentence and ask me to rewrite it, then you critique.

Sure. Rewrite this sentence to be clearer while keeping the meaning and a formal tone: “By foregrounding the ostensibly neutral criterion of clarity, evaluative practices may inadvertently instantiate normative expectations that function to delimit legitimate scholarly voice.” Try one or two versions, and I’ll critique them.

Version 1: “When evaluators treat clarity as a neutral criterion, they may unintentionally impose norms that limit which scholarly voices are seen as legitimate.” Version 2: “Foregrounding clarity as a supposedly neutral standard can lead evaluators to enforce norms that restrict legitimate scholarly voice.”

Both are strong. Quick critique and tweaks: Version 1 strengths: - Clear agent (“evaluators”). - Good hedge (“may” + “unintentionally”). - Concrete verb (“impose norms”). Minor tweak: “which scholarly voices” is good, but you can tighten: “When evaluators treat clarity as neutral, they may unintentionally impose norms that determine which voices count as legitimate scholarship.” Version 2 strengths: - Keeps the nominal “foregrounding” but uses it effectively. - “Supposedly” captures “ostensibly” well. Minor tweak: “lead evaluators to enforce” is slightly indirect; you can make it more direct: “Treating clarity as a supposedly neutral standard can encourage evaluators to enforce norms that restrict which voices count as legitimate.” Takeaway: you preserved the conceptual content (neutrality claim → norm enforcement → legitimacy boundary) while making the causal chain explicit. That’s exactly the move.
I notice you use “count as” a lot. Is that okay, or is it too casual?

“Count as” is slightly informal, but it’s also a precise philosophical/rhetorical phrase for category membership (“what qualifies as”). In many academic contexts it’s acceptable. If you want a more formal alternative, you can vary: - “qualify as” - “be recognized as” - “be regarded as” - “be treated as” - “be considered” Example: “…norms that shape which voices are recognized as legitimate scholarship.” If you’re worried about register, use “recognized as” or “regarded as.” But don’t avoid a phrase solely because it’s common; common phrases can be the clearest.
Another thing: I sometimes write paragraphs that start with a citation. I was taught that’s fine. But I’m told it reads like a list. How do I fix that?

Starting with a citation isn’t inherently wrong, but it often signals that the paragraph is organized around sources rather than around your claim. The fix is to lead with your synthesis claim, then cite. List-like: “Author A argues X. Author B argues Y. Author C argues Z.” Synthesis-led: “Scholars disagree about whether clarity primarily serves comprehension or enforcement. Author A treats clarity as ____; Author B emphasizes ____; Author C reframes ____.” Even better: use citations as evidence for your sentence rather than as the sentence’s subject. “Clarity is often framed as reader accessibility (A; B), but this framing has been criticized as a normative demand that can marginalize non-dominant styles (C; D).” Practical rule: - Begin paragraphs with your voice (your claim). - Use citations to support, complicate, or exemplify that claim. This reads more authoritative and less like a bibliography in prose.
How do I avoid repeating the same sentence openings? Mine often start with “This” or “However.”

Repetition is only a problem when it becomes monotonous or ambiguous. You can diversify openings by varying what you put in subject position. Options: 1) Use a key term as subject “Clarity functions as…” “Evaluation practices shape…” 2) Use a gerund phrase (sparingly) “Treating clarity as neutral obscures…” 3) Use a prepositional opener (sparingly; keep short) “In evaluative contexts, clarity often…” 4) Use a contrastive opener beyond “However” “Yet,” “By contrast,” “Nevertheless,” “At the same time,” 5) Use a question (rarely, but effective at section starts) “What, then, counts as clarity in practice?” 6) Use a dependent clause to signal logic “Although clarity is often framed as accessibility, …” But: don’t contort sentences just to avoid “This.” Better: keep “This” but attach a noun: “This assumption…” “This framing…” “This distinction…” That solves both monotony and ambiguity.
I’d like to practice revising a whole paragraph for clarity while keeping it academic. Can you give me a dense paragraph and then show a revision, plus explain decisions?

Dense paragraph (practice text): “Scholarly discussions of academic style frequently mobilize clarity as a desideratum, yet the criteria by which clarity is adjudicated remain undertheorized, particularly insofar as such criteria are embedded within evaluative infrastructures that naturalize specific communicative norms. Consequently, pedagogical prescriptions that emphasize ‘plain language’ risk reproducing the very hierarchies they seek to mitigate, insofar as the stylistic features that are taken to index clarity are unevenly distributed and differentially sanctioned across contexts. A more productive approach, therefore, entails a reflexive account of how clarity is operationalized in assessment, alongside an articulation of the conditions under which clarity enhances comprehension rather than conformity.” Revision (formal, clearer): “Academic discussions often treat clarity as a central goal, but they rarely specify how clarity is judged—especially when judgments occur within evaluation systems that present particular communicative norms as neutral. This gap matters for pedagogy. Advice to use ‘plain language’ can reproduce the hierarchies it aims to reduce, because the features treated as signs of clarity are not evenly valued across contexts and audiences. A more productive approach is to examine how assessments operationalize clarity and to identify when clarity improves comprehension rather than enforcing conformity.” Decision notes: - Sentence 1: kept the contrast (“goal” vs “underspecified criteria”) and made the “especially” clause concrete (“evaluation systems”). - Sentence 2: added a short “This gap matters for pedagogy” to make the stakes explicit; it also creates rhythm. - Sentence 3: replaced “insofar as” with “because” and split the long causal chain into two clauses. - Sentence 4: kept the key conceptual distinction (“comprehension vs conformity”) and used parallel verbs (“examine,” “identify”). If you want, you can try your own revision of the dense paragraph first, and I’ll give feedback. Or we can do it collaboratively line by line.

I’ll try. My revision: “Academic discussions often value clarity, but they rarely explain how clarity is judged, especially within evaluation systems that treat certain norms as neutral. This matters for teaching. Advice about ‘plain language’ can reinforce hierarchies because what counts as clear varies by context and is not valued equally. A better approach is to reflect on how clarity is assessed and when it supports comprehension rather than conformity.”

This is excellent—very close to the model revision, and your choices are strong. A few refinements to keep it formal and precise: 1) “value clarity” vs “treat clarity as a goal” “Value” can sound subjective; “treat as” signals discourse analysis. Option: “Academic discussions often treat clarity as a central goal…” 2) “reflect on” vs “analyze/examine” “Reflect” can sound informal or introspective. Option: “A better approach is to examine how clarity is assessed…” 3) Tighten sentence 3 for specificity “what counts as clear varies by context” is good. You can add “audience” to connect to evaluation: “…varies across contexts and audiences…” Revised version of your revision: “Academic discussions often treat clarity as a central goal, but they rarely explain how clarity is judged, especially within evaluation systems that treat particular norms as neutral. This matters for teaching. Advice to use ‘plain language’ can reinforce hierarchies, because what counts as clear varies across contexts and audiences and is not valued equally. A better approach is to examine how clarity is assessed and to specify when it supports comprehension rather than conformity.” Your draft already did the hard work: clear structure, explicit stakes, and preserved nuance.
Great. Now, I want guidance on “topic sentences.” People say mine are too vague. What makes a good topic sentence in academic argument?

A good topic sentence does more than announce the topic; it states the paragraph’s claim and signals how it relates to the thesis. Common weak topic sentence types: - Pure topic: “Clarity is an important concept in academic writing.” - Empty emphasis: “It is important to consider clarity.” - Vague contrast: “However, there are challenges.” Stronger topic sentence patterns: 1) Claim + reason “Clarity is often treated as a neutral standard because evaluators use it as shorthand for competence.” 2) Claim + contrast “Although clarity is typically framed as accessibility, in evaluation contexts it often functions as a norm-enforcement tool.” 3) Claim + scope “In peer review contexts, clarity judgments tend to track disciplinary expectations more than reader comprehension.” 4) Claim + consequence “When clarity is equated with a single style, it can marginalize legitimate forms of scholarly voice.” 5) Claim + question the paragraph answers “To understand why dense prose is rewarded, we must examine how expertise is signaled in evaluation.” A quick self-check: After your topic sentence, can you add “This paragraph shows this by…”? If yes, it’s likely a claim.
Sometimes my paragraphs are long because I’m trying to be thorough. How long should a paragraph be, and when should I split it?

There’s no universal length, but in argument-driven prose, paragraphs often work best when they handle one claim with enough support. Split when: - you shift from one claim to another, - you move from summary to critique to your stance (three moves might need two paragraphs), - you change the comparative axis (definitions → implications), - the reader would benefit from a pause before a new step in the logic. Practical heuristics: - If a paragraph exceeds ~200–250 words, check whether it contains multiple sub-claims. - If you have more than ~3 citations serving different purposes, consider splitting into clusters. - If your topic sentence no longer describes the whole paragraph by the end, split. How to split without losing flow: - End the first paragraph with a mini-synthesis. - Begin the next with a transition that names the next move (“This raises a further issue…”). Thoroughness can be achieved via a sequence of shorter, logically connected paragraphs rather than one long block.
I also get comments that my writing is “abstract” and needs “concrete examples.” But in conceptual writing, what counts as an example?

In conceptual writing, examples don’t have to be empirical datasets. They can be: - a brief hypothetical scenario, - a representative quotation or paraphrase of a common evaluative comment, - a mini case: two contrasting sentences showing “dense vs clear,” - a stylized vignette of a typical situation (kept generic), - a short classification table (in text) of types. Examples help by anchoring abstractions to something the reader can visualize. For your topic, examples could include: - Two versions of the same claim: one jargon-heavy, one clear, showing that clarity doesn’t remove complexity. - An example of an evaluative statement (“This is unclear” vs “This lacks a defined scope”), illustrating operationalization. - A “before/after” revision showing how to preserve hedging while reducing clutter. A good example is short and explicitly connected: “For instance, when evaluators praise ‘clarity,’ they often mean X—e.g., ‘the argument is easy to follow because…’—rather than Y.”
How do I incorporate examples without derailing the flow or sounding like I’m teaching basics?

Treat examples as evidence for a claim, not as a detour. Introduce them with a purpose statement and keep them compact. A clean integration pattern: Claim → “For example…” → Example → Interpretation → Return to claim Example: “Clarity is often treated as a proxy for expertise. For example, evaluative comments may praise ‘sophisticated’ writing when it uses specialized terms without defining them. This suggests that clarity judgments can reward signaling over comprehension. Recognizing this dynamic helps explain why calls for ‘plain language’ sometimes meet resistance.” Also: you can signal that the example is illustrative, not remedial: “A brief example clarifies the distinction.” “A simple illustration shows how the mechanism works.” That maintains scholarly tone.
I want to reduce “empty phrases.” Can you give me a list of common empty phrases in academic writing and what to replace them with?

Common empty or low-information phrases (and replacements): - “It is important to note that…” → state the point directly; or “Crucially,” if needed. - “It should be noted that…” → delete. - “In today’s society…” → specify context or delete. - “Since the beginning of time…” → delete. - “In terms of…” → “regarding,” “for,” “in,” or rewrite with a verb. - “With respect to…” → “for,” “about,” or rewrite. - “In relation to…” → “about,” “under,” “when,” “because,” depending on meaning. - “The fact that…” → usually delete: “That X occurs…” - “It is widely known that…” → cite or delete. - “Needless to say…” → delete. - “At the end of the day…” → delete. - “As previously mentioned…” → delete or “As discussed above,” (rare). - “There is a need for more research…” → specify what research and why: “Future work should test… because…” Replacement principle: replace a filler phrase with either (a) nothing, or (b) a specific logical connector (“because,” “however,” “therefore”), or (c) a specific verb (“analyze,” “distinguish,” “demonstrate”).
Sometimes my writing has “there is/are” a lot. Is that a problem?

“There is/are” (existential constructions) aren’t inherently wrong, but overuse can make prose static and delay the real subject. When to keep: - When you genuinely want to assert existence: “There are two dominant approaches…” When to revise: - When it hides the actor or main idea: “There are many scholars who argue…” → “Many scholars argue…” Quick fixes: - Replace “There is/are + noun” with the noun as subject: “There is a tension between…” → “A tension exists between…” (or better) “These accounts conflict because…” - Replace “There is evidence that…” → “Evidence suggests that…” or “Several studies show that…” The goal is to put the concept doing the work in subject position and use a strong verb.
How do I balance being concise with being precise? Sometimes when I cut words, I lose nuance.

Concision and precision can coexist if you cut redundancy but keep scope conditions and key distinctions. Three ways to cut without losing nuance: 1) Cut redundant pairs - “each and every” → “each” - “various different” → “various” - “basic fundamentals” → “fundamentals” 2) Replace phrases with single precise words (carefully) - “a large number of” → “many” - “due to the fact that” → “because” - “in the event that” → “if” 3) Preserve nuance via structure, not extra adjectives Instead of piling qualifiers: “It is somewhat potentially problematic…” Use a condition: “This is problematic when X, because Y.” Also, consider “strategic expansion”: sometimes adding a short clarifying sentence increases precision more than adding qualifiers to an existing sentence. Cutting isn’t always the answer; distributing information often is.
I’ve been told to use “strong verbs.” Can you give examples of weak verbs and stronger alternatives in academic prose?

Weak verbs often rely on “be” (is/are) plus abstract nouns, or vague verbs like “do,” “get,” “have,” “make.” Stronger verbs specify the action. Common upgrades: - “is” (as main verb) → “constitutes,” “represents,” “reflects,” “depends on” (only if accurate) - “has an effect on” → “shapes,” “influences,” “constrains,” “drives” - “is related to” → “correlates with,” “depends on,” “stems from,” “aligns with” (choose carefully) - “shows” → “demonstrates,” “indicates,” “reveals,” “suggests” - “talks about” → “addresses,” “examines,” “critiques,” “theorizes” - “leads to” → “produces,” “generates,” “results in,” “contributes to” (pick strength) - “is about” → “concerns,” “focuses on,” “analyzes” Caution: don’t swap in fancy verbs just to sound academic. “Show” is often perfectly good. The “strength” should come from specificity, not grandeur.
Could you show an example where a “strong verb” actually makes things less accurate?

Yes—over-strong verbs can overstate causality or certainty. Example: Weak but safe: “These findings suggest that evaluation practices influence writing style.” Over-strong and possibly inaccurate: “These findings prove that evaluation practices determine writing style.” “Prove” implies definitive demonstration; “determine” implies near-total causation. If your evidence is correlational or interpretive, that’s too strong. Another: Safe: “The data are consistent with the hypothesis that…” Too strong: “The data confirm that…” So: choose verbs that match evidential strength. “Suggest,” “indicate,” “support,” “are consistent with” are often more accurate than “prove,” “demonstrate conclusively,” “establish.”
I also struggle with “voice.” Some people say my writing doesn’t sound like me. But academic writing feels like it requires a mask.

Academic voice doesn’t have to be a mask; it can be a disciplined version of your thinking. “Voice” in academic writing often means: - a clear stance (what you claim and why), - consistent terms and commitments, - a recognizable way of moving through ideas (definitions, distinctions, counterarguments). You can sound like yourself by: 1) Using your natural clarity preferences while keeping formality If you naturally write plainly, that can be your voice—paired with precise concepts. 2) Making your commitments explicit “I argue…” “I distinguish…” “I use X to mean…” This is not informal; it’s responsible authorship. 3) Avoiding borrowed tics Phrases like “it is worth noting” or “insofar as” often come from imitating academic style. Replace with your own straightforward logic markers. 4) Allowing controlled variation Occasional short sentences for emphasis, occasional longer ones for synthesis. If you want, we can craft a “style guide” for your own writing: a set of preferred transitions, hedges, and verbs that match your voice but stay scholarly.
I like that idea. What would a personal style guide include?

A personal style guide can be short—1–2 pages—and cover decisions you repeatedly face. Sections to include: 1) Core principles (3–5) - “Lead with claims, not citations.” - “One main idea per sentence; nuance in a second sentence.” - “Prefer ‘because/therefore’ over ‘in terms of.’” 2) Preferred vocabulary - List key terms and your preferred phrasing (e.g., “evaluation system” not “evaluative regime,” unless you mean something specific). - A list of “avoid unless necessary” words (e.g., “problematize,” “valorize”). 3) Transition bank - Contrast: “However,” “Yet,” “By contrast” - Extension: “Moreover,” “Building on this” - Qualification: “In contexts where…” - Stakes: “This matters because…” 4) Hedging policy - Use “tend to” for general patterns. - Use “can” for capacity. - Use “may” sparingly; avoid stacking. 5) Sentence templates you like - “Although X, Y.” - “X matters because Y.” - “This approach assumes ____, whereas ____.” Templates reduce cognitive load during drafting. 6) Revision checklist tailored to your habits - “Delete ‘it is important to note.’” - “Replace ‘in relation to’ with a specific relation.” If you tell me 2–3 phrases you overuse and 2–3 you admire in others’ writing, we can draft a style guide that’s genuinely yours.

Phrases I overuse: “insofar as,” “with respect to,” “it is worth noting,” “in relation to.” Phrases I admire: “Put differently,” “This distinction matters because,” “At the same time,” and I like when writers say “The upshot is…”

Here’s a draft mini style guide aligned with that: Core principles 1) Prefer explicit logical connectors (“because,” “therefore,” “however”) over vague relational phrases. 2) Use metadiscourse only to orient (definitions, scope, roadmap), not to inflate. 3) Keep one hedge per claim; put nuance into scope conditions. 4) End paragraphs with synthesis or implication (“The upshot is…”). Preferred replacements (your overused phrases) - “insofar as” → “because,” “to the extent that” (only when degree matters), or restructure. - “with respect to” → “for,” “regarding,” “in,” or name the relationship (“in evaluations of…”). - “it is worth noting” → delete, or “Crucially,” or better: state consequence (“This matters because…”). - “in relation to” → “under,” “when,” “compared with,” “because of,” depending on intended relation. Signature moves (phrases you admire) - Use “Put differently,” when you reframe, not when you repeat. - Use “This distinction matters because…” to connect concept to stakes. - Use “At the same time,” to signal legitimate tension (not as a filler). - Use “The upshot is…” to close sections with a clear implication. Transition bank (sample) - Contrast: “However,” “Yet,” “By contrast,” “Nevertheless” - Reframing: “Put differently,” “In other words,” - Tension: “At the same time,” “Even so,” - Stakes: “This matters because,” “The upshot is,” Hedging policy - Prefer: “tends to,” “often,” “in some contexts” - Avoid stacking: “arguably,” “perhaps,” “potentially” together If you adopt this consistently, your prose will sound both more like you and more controlled.
Nice. Now, can we talk about “jargon” specifically? Sometimes I need specialized terms, but I want to avoid being opaque. How do I introduce specialized terminology smoothly?

Introduce specialized terms with (1) a plain-language paraphrase, (2) a precise definition, and (3) a quick example—then reuse the term consistently. A smooth introduction template: “I use [TERM] to refer to [plain paraphrase], specifically [precise definition]. For example, [brief example].” Example: “I use ‘operationalization’ to refer to how an abstract concept is translated into criteria for judgment, specifically the indicators evaluators use to decide whether writing is ‘clear.’ For example, an evaluator might treat explicit definitions and signposted transitions as indicators of clarity.” Then: - Don’t re-define every time. - Don’t introduce multiple new terms in one paragraph. - If a term is controversial, acknowledge that briefly: “Although ‘clarity’ is often treated as self-evident, I use it here to mean…” Also: consider whether you can postpone the term until after the reader understands the concept. Sometimes you can explain first, label second.
How do I handle counterarguments without getting lost in long “on the one hand/on the other hand” tangles?

Treat counterarguments as structured moves, not as an open-ended balancing act. A clean pattern: 1) State your claim. 2) Present the strongest counterargument fairly (steelman). 3) Concede what it gets right (limited concession). 4) Explain why your claim still holds (rebuttal or refinement). 5) State the revised claim (often more precise). Template: “One might object that ____. This objection is persuasive insofar as ____. However, it overlooks ____. Therefore, ____.” Example: “One might object that emphasizing clarity encourages oversimplification. This concern is persuasive insofar as some ‘clarity’ guidelines discourage necessary qualification. However, clarity need not mean removing nuance; it can mean making inferential steps explicit. Therefore, the issue is not clarity per se but how clarity is defined and evaluated.” This keeps you from spiraling. Also: keep counterarguments to one paragraph unless they are central; otherwise they can dominate.
Sometimes I write “However” too often in counterarguments and it sounds combative. Any alternatives?

Alternatives depend on the relationship: Soft contrast: - “Yet” - “Even so” - “At the same time” - “Still” Qualification: - “That said” - “Nevertheless” - “Even if” Correction: - “Instead” - “Rather” - “More precisely” Partial agreement: - “While this is true, …” - “This is right to emphasize…, but…” You can also reduce explicit contrast markers by using structure: - Separate paragraphs: one for counterargument, one for response. - Use topic sentences that signal the move: “A stronger objection concerns…” Combative tone often comes less from “however” and more from dismissive phrasing (“clearly mistaken”). Keep your critique specific and evidence-based.
I also have trouble with “flow” at the sentence level. Sometimes even revised sentences feel awkward. What should I look for?

Sentence-level flow often hinges on information order: old information first, new information last. Readers prefer when a sentence starts with something they recognize and ends with what’s being added. Techniques: 1) Given-to-new ordering Bad flow: “Normative expectations are instantiated by evaluative practices.” Better: “Evaluative practices can instantiate normative expectations.” 2) Consistent topics across sentences (topic string) “Clarity…” “This standard…” “These judgments…” 3) Avoid long “wind-up” before the main verb If the reader waits too long to find the action, flow suffers. 4) Put heavy material at the end Short subject, strong verb, then details. 5) Read aloud for stress points Awkwardness often shows up as places you naturally want to pause. If you have an awkward sentence (invented is fine), paste it and I’ll show two alternative word orders and explain why one flows better.
Invented awkward sentence: “In the context of evaluative commentary that is produced under conditions of time pressure, clarity as a criterion is frequently invoked.”

Two clearer, better-flowing options: Option A (active, given-to-new): “Under time pressure, evaluators often invoke clarity as a criterion in their comments.” Option B (slightly more formal, still clear): “When writing comments under time pressure, evaluators frequently invoke clarity as a criterion.” Why these flow better: - Start with the condition (“under time pressure”) then quickly name the agent (“evaluators”). - Main verb (“invoke”) appears early. - “in their comments” is a light add-on; you can include it if needed. Your original delays the agent and the action with stacked prepositional phrases (“in the context of… under conditions of…”). The content is fine; it just needs a shorter runway.
Good. Another issue: I sometimes use synonyms to avoid repetition, but then readers say my terms are inconsistent. How should I handle repetition?

In academic writing, repetition is often a virtue when it preserves conceptual consistency. Avoid “elegant variation” for key terms. Guidelines: - Repeat key terms (central concepts, variables, categories) consistently. - Vary only for genuinely interchangeable words that don’t carry conceptual weight (e.g., “paper/article,” “discuss/examine” sometimes). - If you must vary, signal equivalence: “evaluation systems (hereafter, evaluative infrastructures)” But do this sparingly; it can also add burden. A useful distinction: - Conceptual repetition (good): repeating “clarity,” “evaluation,” “norms.” - Stylistic repetition (can vary): repeating “important,” “interesting,” “very.” If a reader needs to track an argument, consistent terminology is more helpful than stylistic variety.
Could you talk about “paragraph cohesion” with an example? Like how to keep all sentences pulling in the same direction.

Cohesion comes from a clear controlling idea and from each sentence either (a) supporting it, (b) elaborating it, or (c) qualifying it in a way that still advances the point. Example controlling idea: “Clarity judgments often function as proxies for expertise in evaluation.” Cohesive paragraph: “Clarity judgments often function as proxies for expertise in evaluation. Rather than assessing whether an argument can be followed, evaluators may treat specialized terminology as evidence that the writer ‘belongs’ in a scholarly conversation. This proxy logic helps explain why feedback sometimes praises dense prose while criticizing accessible prose as ‘too simple.’ At the same time, the proxy is not inevitable: when evaluators are asked to justify clarity assessments with specific criteria (e.g., defined terms, explicit claims), their comments tend to shift toward comprehension-focused measures.” Why cohesive: - Sentence 1 states claim. - Sentence 2 explains mechanism. - Sentence 3 gives consequence/example. - Sentence 4 qualifies and points to a condition under which the pattern changes. If a sentence doesn’t fit one of those roles, it may belong elsewhere.
I see. I also have trouble with introductions. I either write a huge abstract intro or I start too narrow. How do I structure an intro for an argument paper?

A common, effective intro structure for argument papers is: 1) Problem/tension (why this matters) 2) Gap (what’s missing or unresolved) 3) Your claim (thesis) 4) Contribution (what your argument does) 5) Roadmap (how the paper proceeds) You can do this in 4–7 paragraphs depending on length. The key is to avoid spending too long in (1) without reaching (3). A compact template: - Paragraph 1: Set up the tension. “Clarity is widely valued, yet it is often treated as self-evident and apolitical.” - Paragraph 2: Explain the stakes and gap. “Because clarity is used in evaluation, unclear definitions can reproduce inequities. Existing accounts either emphasize accessibility or critique clarity as ideology, but they rarely connect these views to concrete assessment practices.” - Paragraph 3: Thesis + contribution. “I argue that clarity should be treated as a set of distinct practices rather than a single standard. Distinguishing comprehension-oriented clarity from conformity-oriented clarity clarifies why calls for ‘plain language’ can both help and harm.” - Paragraph 4: Roadmap. “I first review… then… finally…” If your intros feel abstract, it often means the stakes and the gap are not anchored in a concrete situation (e.g., “evaluation comments,” “teaching guidelines,” “review criteria”). You can keep it general while naming the site of the problem.
Can you show an example intro paragraph that’s formal, clear, and not full of grand claims?

Example intro paragraph: “Clarity is a standard praise and critique in academic writing, yet what counts as ‘clear’ is rarely specified. Because clarity is often invoked in evaluation—whether in feedback, review, or assessment—its ambiguity matters: writers may be asked to meet a standard that is presented as neutral but applied unevenly. This paper argues that clarity is best understood not as a single stylistic ideal but as a set of practices that can serve different functions. Distinguishing clarity that supports comprehension from clarity that enforces conformity helps explain why some clarity advice improves communication while other advice reproduces gatekeeping norms.” This avoids grandiosity by: - keeping the scope to “academic writing” and “evaluation,” - stating a specific reason ambiguity matters, - making a bounded claim (“best understood as…”), - previewing a concrete distinction.
How do I write conclusions that don’t just repeat the intro?

A strong conclusion does more than restate; it synthesizes and extends. Conclusion moves: 1) Re-state thesis in light of what you showed (slightly richer) 2) Summarize key steps (not all details) 3) Articulate implications (so what) 4) Note limitations or conditions (optional) 5) Offer a forward-looking question or recommendation (optional) Template: “This paper argued _____. By showing _____ and _____, it clarifies _____. The upshot is _____. Future work/practice should _____.” Example: “This paper argued that clarity is a contested practice rather than a single standard. By distinguishing comprehension-oriented clarity from conformity-oriented clarity, and by tracing how evaluation systems reward particular stylistic signals, it clarifies why calls for ‘plain language’ can both support understanding and reinforce gatekeeping. The upshot is that teaching and assessing clarity requires explicit criteria and attention to audience and context, not simply a preference for one style. Future work should examine how evaluators justify clarity judgments and how alternative criteria might better align assessment with comprehension.” This feels like an ending because it names implications and recommends action, not just recap.
I like “The upshot is…” but I’m worried it’s too informal. Is it acceptable?

It’s somewhat conversational, but it’s widely used in academic prose, especially in argument-driven writing. If you want more formal alternatives: - “The implication is…” - “The central implication is…” - “The key takeaway is…” - “This suggests that…” - “The result is…” You can also reserve “The upshot is…” for moments where you want a crisp synthesis, and use more formal phrasing elsewhere. Consistency matters: if the overall tone is formal, a single “upshot” won’t break it.
Could you give me a set of “before/after” mini examples that show common density patterns and how to fix them?

Sure—ten mini examples. 1) Nominalization overload Before: “The identification of factors contributing to opacity was undertaken.” After: “We identified factors that make prose opaque.” 2) Long lead-in Before: “In light of the fact that there are multiple perspectives, it is necessary to consider…” After: “Because perspectives differ, we should consider…” 3) Noun stack Before: “clarity assessment criteria formation” After: “how criteria for assessing clarity are formed” 4) Empty metadiscourse Before: “It is important to note that clarity matters.” After: “Clarity matters because it shapes evaluation.” 5) Stacked hedges Before: “It might perhaps be the case that…” After: “It is possible that…” or “In some cases, …” 6) Passive that hides agent Before: “Density is valorized in academic settings.” After: “Reviewers often reward dense prose.” 7) “In terms of” Before: “In terms of pedagogy, clarity is significant.” After: “For teaching, clarity matters.” 8) “Which” ambiguity Before: “Evaluators reward jargon, which reinforces inequity.” After: “Rewarding jargon can reinforce inequity.” 9) Abstract verbs Before: “This framework functions to problematize the notion of clarity.” After: “This framework challenges the idea that clarity is neutral.” 10) Redundant phrasing Before: “a number of different approaches” After: “several approaches” If you want, we can turn these into a self-drill: you rewrite 3–4 dense sentences and I give targeted feedback.
Let’s do a drill. Give me 5 dense sentences, and I’ll rewrite them.

Here are five dense sentences. Rewrite each for clarity while keeping a formal tone and the original meaning. 1) “The privileging of density within scholarly communication can be understood as an outcome of evaluative cultures that conflate terminological specialization with intellectual depth.” 2) “A failure to disambiguate clarity as comprehension from clarity as conformity results in pedagogical guidance that is at once inconsistent and normatively loaded.” 3) “The invocation of neutrality in discussions of style often obscures the situatedness of the standards being applied.” 4) “Although plain-language initiatives purport to increase accessibility, they may inadvertently reinscribe hierarchical distinctions by treating one register as universally appropriate.” 5) “What is at stake is not merely stylistic preference but the distribution of epistemic authority across audiences.”

Rewrites: 1) “Scholarly communication often favors dense writing because evaluative cultures mistake specialized terminology for intellectual depth.” 2) “When clarity as comprehension is not distinguished from clarity as conformity, teaching advice becomes inconsistent and normatively loaded.” 3) “Calling style standards ‘neutral’ often hides the fact that these standards are situated.” 4) “Plain-language initiatives aim to increase accessibility, but they can unintentionally reinforce hierarchies by treating one register as universally appropriate.” 5) “The issue is not just stylistic preference but how epistemic authority is distributed across audiences.”

These are strong—clear, accurate, and still formal. Small refinements: 1) “mistake” is clear but slightly informal/psychological. Alternatives: “treat,” “equate,” “conflate.” Revised: “Scholarly communication often favors dense writing because evaluative cultures equate specialized terminology with intellectual depth.” 2) Excellent. You kept the conceptual distinction intact. 3) Good. If you want slightly more formal than “calling,” use “Invoking.” “Invoking neutrality in discussions of style often obscures the situated nature of the standards being applied.” But your version is fine. 4) Excellent; you preserved the hedge and mechanism. 5) Excellent. “The issue is” is fine; “What is at stake is” is more formal but longer. Your rewrite is clearer. Overall, you’re doing the key moves: agent + verb, fewer nominalizations, explicit causal connectors.
Now I want to work on “sentence spines.” You mentioned subject-verb clarity. Can you explain how to identify the spine and fix it?

The “sentence spine” is the core subject + main verb + object/complement. Dense sentences often bury the spine under introductory phrases, parentheticals, and clauses. How to identify: 1) Find the main verb (not in a subordinate clause). 2) Ask: who/what is doing that verb? (subject) 3) Ask: what is being acted on or asserted? (object/complement) Example dense sentence: “In the context of academic evaluation, the operationalization of clarity through rubrics and commentary has been shown to function as a mechanism for the reproduction of disciplinary norms.” Spine: “Operationalization … functions as a mechanism.” But that’s still nominalized. Fix by making agent/action explicit: “Academic evaluators operationalize clarity through rubrics and commentary, and these practices can reproduce disciplinary norms.” Revision steps: - Move context to a short opener (“In academic evaluation,”). - Convert nominalization (“operationalization”) into verb (“operationalize”). - Put the actor (“evaluators”) in subject position. - Use a concrete verb (“reproduce”) for the effect. A quick self-edit tactic: Underline the spine. If it’s “X is Y” with abstract nouns, consider rewriting with an action verb.
How do I handle long sentences that genuinely need multiple parts? Sometimes one sentence has to carry a complex relationship.

Use “controlled complexity”: one main clause, plus one subordinate clause, plus optional end-loaded modifiers. If you need more, consider splitting. Strategies: 1) Use punctuation to clarify structure - Semicolons can join closely related independent clauses. - Em dashes can add an aside, but use sparingly. - Colons can introduce an explanation or list. 2) Build the sentence around the main claim Put the main claim early; add conditions later. 3) Use parallelism for multi-part relationships “X not only…, but also…” “X depends on A, B, and C.” Example: “Clarity improves comprehension when writers define key terms and signal inferential steps, but it can enforce conformity when evaluators treat one register as universally neutral.” This is complex but readable because it’s a clear contrast with parallel “when” clauses. If a sentence has multiple “because/although/which” clauses, it’s usually a sign to split.
I’ve heard of “reader expectations” like “topic position” and “stress position.” Can you explain that?

Yes—this comes from principles of information structure. Readers expect: - The beginning of a sentence (topic position) to connect to what came before. - The end of a sentence (stress position) to carry new or emphasized information. So, to improve clarity: - Put familiar concepts early. - Put the key new point at the end. Example (less effective): “Because evaluators often work under time pressure, clarity is invoked as a criterion.” Better (stronger stress position): “Evaluators often invoke clarity as a criterion, especially under time pressure.” Now “under time pressure” is an added condition, but the main point lands at “criterion.” Or: “We argue that clarity is contested.” If the key point is “contested,” end there. A practical edit: For each sentence, ask: what is the “punchline”? Put it near the end. Then make sure the beginning links back to the previous sentence’s topic.
This is very helpful, but I worry about over-editing and losing momentum. How do I keep drafting productive while still improving clarity?

Separate drafting from editing, and give yourself constraints. Drafting practices: - Draft fast with placeholders: “[define later]” “[example]” instead of stopping to perfect. - Write “rough topic sentences” first: one claim per paragraph, then fill in. - Allow messy sentences; your job is to get the logic down. Editing practices: - Do a reverse outline before line edits. - Limit sentence-level editing to one pass focused on your top 2–3 issues (e.g., nominalizations + hedges). - Set a time box (e.g., 30 minutes) for line editing per section. Momentum-preserving constraint: During drafting, ban yourself from rewriting the same sentence more than twice. Mark it and move on. Then fix in the sentence-level pass. Clarity is easier to achieve when the argument structure is stable. If you over-edit early, you’ll keep rearranging and never finish.
Could you propose a weekly practice plan to steadily improve clarity? Like exercises I can do.

A weekly plan (30–45 minutes per session, 4 sessions/week): Session 1: Reverse outline practice - Take 2–3 pages of your writing. - Write a one-sentence summary for each paragraph. - Rewrite any paragraph topic sentence that doesn’t match its summary. Session 2: Sentence de-densifying - Select 10 sentences. - For each: underline the spine, circle nominalizations, bracket prepositional chains. - Rewrite to restore verbs and reduce chains. Session 3: Synthesis paragraph drill - Take 3 source claims. - Write a paragraph that leads with your synthesis, cites multiple sources, ends with “This tension suggests…” - Focus on relational verbs: “contrasts,” “extends,” “complicates.” Session 4: Style consistency - Make a list of 10 “preferred” phrases and 10 “avoid” phrases. - Do a search-and-replace audit on a draft: replace “in terms of,” “insofar as,” etc. - Check for consistent key terms. Optional add-on: Once a week, read one page aloud and mark where you stumble; revise only those spots. If you want, tell me your top two recurring comments from feedback, and I’ll tailor the plan to them.
Recurring comments: “unclear what your claim is” and “too much jargon/too abstract.” I think I hide my thesis.

Tailored plan for those comments: 1) “Unclear claim” fix: claim-first drafting - Before writing a section, write a 1–2 sentence claim that answers “What do I want the reader to believe after this section?” - Start each paragraph with a mini-claim that supports that section claim. Exercise (2x/week): - Take a paragraph and rewrite the first sentence so it includes a claim + relationship marker (“because,” “however,” “therefore”). - Then ensure the rest of the paragraph supports it. 2) “Too abstract/jargon” fix: concept-to-example pairing Exercise (2x/week): - For each key concept you introduce, add one sentence of grounding: a brief example, a contrast, or a practical implication. - Rule: no paragraph with more than two abstract sentences in a row without an anchor. Thesis visibility checklist: - [ ] Thesis appears by end of intro (not later). - [ ] Thesis has a “because” clause (reason) or a “by” clause (method): “I argue X by doing Y.” - [ ] Each section begins with a sentence that explicitly advances thesis (not just “In this section…”). - [ ] Each section ends with a synthesis sentence that restates what was established. If you tend to hide the thesis behind “This paper will explore…,” rewrite it as “I argue…” + the key distinction/mechanism.
Speaking of “I argue,” is first person okay? I’ve been told to avoid it, but also told it’s clearer.

First person is often clearer in argument-driven writing because it marks agency and responsibility for claims. Whether it’s acceptable depends on conventions, but in many academic styles it’s fine. Use first person for: - your argument moves: “I argue,” “I show,” “I distinguish,” “I define” - methodological choices in conceptual work: “I focus on,” “I use X to mean” Avoid first person when: - it’s unnecessary or repetitive: “I think” “I believe” (often weaker) - it replaces evidence: “I feel that…” If you want a compromise: - Use “this paper argues” (still clear, slightly more impersonal). But “I argue” is usually the clearest and most direct. You can also mix: first person for key contributions, third person for general statements.
How do I make sure my “I argue” sentences don’t sound arrogant?

Arrogance usually comes from overclaiming or dismissing alternatives, not from first person itself. To keep it appropriately modest: - Bound your claim: “I argue that X is best understood as…” rather than “I prove…” - Specify context: “In evaluative contexts…” “In this discussion…” - Acknowledge limits: “This account does not claim…” - Use evidence-based verbs: “I show,” “I demonstrate” only if you actually do. Example: “I argue that clarity in evaluation is best understood as a set of practices with distinct functions, rather than as a single neutral standard.” That’s confident but not arrogant because it’s framed as an interpretation and it defines scope.
I want to learn to edit for “reader burden.” How do I identify where the reader is working too hard?

Look for places where the reader must hold too many things in working memory at once. Signs: - long sentences with multiple embedded clauses, - pronouns with unclear referents, - abstract nouns without concrete anchors, - sudden term switches, - paragraphs that change direction mid-way. Practical diagnostics: 1) Highlight “new terms” If a paragraph introduces many new terms without definitions/examples, burden increases. 2) Mark “referent” clarity Circle every “this/it/they/which.” If you can’t identify the referent instantly, revise. 3) Identify “logic gaps” Where you imply causality without stating it. Add “because/therefore.” 4) Use the “one-breath test” Read aloud. If you can’t read a sentence in one breath, it may need splitting or restructuring. 5) Ask “What question is this sentence answering?” If you can’t answer, it might be filler or off-track. Reader burden decreases when you (a) name agents, (b) use explicit logic, and (c) anchor abstractions.
Okay, last big thing: I want my writing to be clear but still “theoretical.” How do I write theory without sounding like I’m hiding behind obscurity?

Theoretical writing is clear when it makes its abstractions operational for the reader: it defines terms, states claims, clarifies relations, and shows what follows. Principles for clear theory: 1) Define your core terms early and reuse them consistently. 2) State your theoretical claim in ordinary language first; then provide the formal/theory-loaded articulation. 3) Use diagrams in prose: “X consists of three parts: A, B, C.” 4) Provide a minimal example after each key theoretical move. 5) Distinguish levels: concept (“clarity”), mechanism (“how clarity judgments operate”), and implication (“what this changes about teaching/evaluation”). A useful two-layer sentence pattern: - Layer 1 (plain): “Clarity judgments often track conformity to local norms.” - Layer 2 (theoretical): “Put differently, ‘clarity’ functions as a gatekeeping device that naturalizes a particular register.” This lets readers grasp the claim before encountering the theoretical framing. Clear theory is not less sophisticated; it’s more accountable.
Can you give me a final consolidated set of rules I can keep in mind while revising?

Consolidated revision rules (high-yield): 1) Lead with claims: start paragraphs with what you’re asserting, not with citations or throat-clearing. 2) Make logic explicit: use “because/therefore/however” to show relationships. 3) One sentence, one job: don’t stack multiple concessions, definitions, and claims. 4) Restore verbs: convert nominalizations into actions; name agents when agency matters. 5) Reduce noun stacks and prepositional chains: unpack with verbs and clear relations. 6) Hedge with scope, not fog: “in some contexts” beats “arguably, perhaps.” 7) Define terms cleanly: core definition, contrast, scope—separate sentences. 8) Use consistent key terms: avoid elegant variation for concepts. 9) Anchor abstractions: add brief examples or implications regularly. 10) Revise in passes: argument map → paragraph structure → sentence clarity → polish. If you apply just rules 1–4 consistently, your writing will usually stop being “dense” while staying fully academic.
